reason: The paper computes 'feature importance' for the best RF model 'using the Ranger package' (Sect. 2.2) and plots it in Fig. 2 ('Feature importance based on Random Forest'), never specifying permutation mode, which in tidymodels/ranger workflows without other qualification is standardly the impurity (Gini/variance) mode. The specific top-3 ranking (T2M_2, PS_2, WS2M_1) is not a throwaway detail: Sect. 3.4 singles them out as 'the most significant predictors,' and the Discussion builds a mechanistic story directly from that order ('elevated temperatures and pressure levels in February may signal the onset of favorable growing conditions... wind patterns in January could impact foraging behavior'), so the ranking is load-bearing for a stated interpretive conclusion, not just the generic set of 18 Boruta-retained features. No adequate corroboration of this ranking exists: Boruta only performs zero/non-zero relevance detection against shadow features (it does not validate magnitude/order), and the only other check (Fig. S1, AUC vs. number of features across full/Boruta/top-6 models) is an overall-accuracy comparison across feature subsets, which the rubric explicitly excludes as ranking validation. A single garbled phrase ('...six most influential variables based on Shafigurepley importance scores (see Figure 2)') appears to be a corrupted reference to 'Shapley importance,' verified present verbatim in the official PMC/MDPI HTML as well, but it is never elaborated in Methods, no SHAP values/table/plot are shown anywhere else, and it is used to cite the same Figure 2 that is explicitly captioned 'based on Random Forest' -- so this reads as a leftover drafting artifact rather than a genuine, described SHAP corroboration. Given the ranking is explicitly invoked in a scientific interpretation and no real corroboration method is reported, the flag holds, with the caveat that the paper's silence on impurity-vs-permutation for the ranger call is inferred from common practice rather than stated outright.
